content:
# Luke Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 87-88 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_003_page_001.jpg, LUK_003_page_002.jpg

---

**1** In the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar, while Pontius Pilate was governing Judea, and Herod was tetrarch of Galilee, and his brother Philip was tetrarch of the region of Iturea and Trachonitis, and Lysanias was tetrarch of Abilene,

**2** during the high priesthood of Annas and Caiaphas, the word of God came upon John the son of Zechariah in the wilderness.

**3** And he went into all the region around the Jordan, proclaiming a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins,

**4** as it is written in the book of the words of Isaiah the prophet: "A voice of one crying out in the wilderness: 'Prepare the way of the Lord; make straight his paths.

**5** Every valley shall be filled, and every mountain and hill shall be brought low; the crooked shall become straight, and the rough ways shall become smooth;

**6** and all flesh shall see the salvation of God.'"

**7** Therefore he said to the crowds coming out to be baptized by him, "You offspring of vipers! Who warned you to flee from the coming wrath?

**8** Bring forth therefore fruits worthy of repentance, and do not begin to say within yourselves, 'We have Abraham as our father.' For I say to you that God is able from these stones to raise up children for Abraham.

**9** And already the axe is laid at the root of the trees; every tree therefore that does not bring forth good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire."

**10** And the crowds asked him, saying, "What then shall we do?"

**11** And he answered them, "He who has two tunics, let him share with the one who has none; and he who has food, let him do likewise."

**12** And tax collectors also came to be baptized and said to him, "Teacher, what shall we do?"

**13** And he said to them, "Collect nothing more than what has been appointed to you."

**14** And soldiers also asked him, saying, "And what shall we do, even we?" And he said to them, "Extort no one, nor accuse anyone falsely, and be content with your wages."

**15** Now as the people were in expectation, and all were reasoning in their hearts concerning John, whether perhaps he might be the Christ,

**16** John answered, saying to all, "I indeed baptize you with water; but one mightier than I is coming, the strap of whose sandals I am not worthy to loose. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire,

**17** whose winnowing fork is in his hand to thoroughly cleanse his threshing floor and to gather the wheat into his storehouse, but the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire."

**18** Therefore, exhorting with many other things also, he proclaimed the good news to the people.

**19** But Herod the tetrarch, being reproved by him concerning Herodias the wife of his brother, and concerning all the evil things that Herod had done,

**20** added this also above all: he shut up John in prison.

**21** Now it came to pass, when all the people were being baptized, and Jesus having been baptized and praying, that the heaven was opened,

**22** and the Holy Spirit descended upon him in bodily form as a dove, and a voice came from heaven: "You are my beloved Son; in you I am well pleased."

**23** And Jesus himself was about thirty years of age when he began his ministry, being the son (as was supposed) of Joseph, the son of Heli,

**24** the son of Matthat, the son of Levi, the son of Melchi, the son of Jannai, the son of Joseph,

**25** the son of Mattathias, the son of Amos, the son of Nahum, the son of Esli, the son of Naggai,

**26** the son of Maath, the son of Mattathias, the son of Semein, the son of Josech, the son of Joda,

**27** the son of Joanan, the son of Rhesa, the son of Zerubbabel, the son of Shealtiel, the son of Neri,

**28** the son of Melchi, the son of Addi, the son of Cosam, the son of Elmadam, the son of Er,

**29** the son of Joshua, the son of Eliezer, the son of Jorim, the son of Matthat, the son of Levi,

**30** the son of Simeon, the son of Judah, the son of Joseph, the son of Jonam, the son of Eliakim,

**31** the son of Melea, the son of Menna, the son of Mattatha, the son of Nathan, the son of David,

**32** the son of Jesse, the son of Obed, the son of Boaz, the son of Sala, the son of Nahshon,

**33** the son of Amminadab, the son of Admin, the son of Arni, the son of Hezron, the son of Perez, the son of Judah,

**34** the son of Jacob, the son of Isaac, the son of Abraham, the son of Terah, the son of Nahor,

**35** the son of Serug, the son of Reu, the son of Peleg, the son of Eber, the son of Shelah,

**36** the son of Cainan, the son of Arphaxad, the son of Shem, the son of Noah, the son of Lamech,

**37** the son of Methuselah, the son of Enoch, the son of Jared, the son of Mahalaleel, the son of Cainan,

**38** the son of Enosh, the son of Seth, the son of Adam, the son of God.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Areas of Full Agreement (All Three Translations)
The genealogy in verses 24-38 is effectively identical across all three translations, with only the stylistic difference that EMET brackets "[the son]" while LOGOS uses "the son" and PNEUMA uses "son" alone. SOPHIA adopts "the son of" (LOGOS) as the balanced form that is both natural and faithful to the repeated Greek genitive (tou). All three preserve the distinctive Sinaiticus readings: Admin/Arni in v. 33, Cainan in v. 36, and the full 77-name genealogy from Jesus to God.

All three translations agree on the core content and theological substance of every verse. The heavenly voice in v. 22 follows the standard Sinaiticus reading ("You are my Son, the Beloved; in you I am well pleased") rather than the Western variant from Codex Bezae. All three correctly render the plural "fruits" in v. 8, following the Sinaiticus reading KARPOUS. All three note that Sinaiticus does not name Philip as Herodias's husband in v. 19.

### Consensus Methodology
Where all three substantially agreed, SOPHIA adopted the shared reading. Where two of three agreed, the majority reading was preferred unless the minority reading was demonstrably closer to the Greek. Where all three differed in style, SOPHIA favored the rendering that best combined accuracy, clarity, and literary dignity. LOGOS's formal approach was most frequently adopted as the baseline, with EMET correcting toward literalness where needed and PNEUMA improving readability where possible without sacrificing precision.

### Verse-by-Verse Decisions of Note

**v. 1:** LOGOS and PNEUMA both restructure the verse into clearer English; EMET preserves the Greek participial chain. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's formal restructuring ("while Pontius Pilate was governing") as it preserves the subordinate structure without being wooden.

**v. 2:** EMET renders EPI as "upon" for the high priesthood; LOGOS uses "during"; PNEUMA restructures entirely. SOPHIA adopts "during" (LOGOS) as the most accurate English equivalent of EPI + genitive in temporal use, while keeping "came upon" for the word coming to John, preserving the directional EPI + accusative that all three note.

**v. 3:** All three agree on substance. PNEUMA paraphrases "calling people to be baptized as a sign that they had turned from their sins," which is interpretive. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "proclaiming a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness of sins."

**v. 4:** EMET: "has been written"; LOGOS: "is written"; PNEUMA: "fulfilled what was written." SOPHIA uses "is written" (Greek perfect gegraptai, standard rendering). PNEUMA's "fulfilled" is an interpretive addition not in the text.

**v. 6:** PNEUMA renders "all flesh" as "all humanity." SOPHIA retains "all flesh" (EMET/LOGOS) as the biblical idiom that Luke deliberately employs.

**v. 7:** EMET: "Offspring of vipers"; LOGOS: "You offspring of vipers"; PNEUMA: "You brood of vipers!" SOPHIA uses "You offspring of vipers!" — "offspring" is closer to GENNHMATA than "brood," while adding PNEUMA's exclamation mark for the rhetorical force.

**v. 8:** EMET: "Produce fruits"; LOGOS: "Bring forth fruits"; PNEUMA: "Produce fruit that proves you have genuinely changed." SOPHIA uses "Bring forth fruits worthy of repentance" (LOGOS) as faithful and dignified. PNEUMA's paraphrase, while vivid, departs from the source. EMET's "out of these stones" vs. LOGOS's "from these stones" — SOPHIA uses "from" as more natural while preserving EK.

**v. 11:** EMET uses "tunics/foods" (plural); LOGOS uses "tunics/food"; PNEUMA restructures to conditional sentences. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's phrasing as balanced.

**v. 15:** EMET: "deliberating"; LOGOS: "reasoning"; PNEUMA: "wondering." SOPHIA uses "reasoning" (LOGOS) as closest to DIALOGIZOMENON.

**v. 17:** EMET: "grain/storehouse"; LOGOS: "wheat/storehouse"; PNEUMA: "wheat/storehouse." SOPHIA uses "wheat" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority), as SITON specifically means wheat.

**v. 21:** PNEUMA renders "heaven was torn open" (using Mark's stronger verb); EMET/LOGOS correctly use "opened" for Sinaiticus's ANOIXQHNAI. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS.

**v. 22:** EMET: "in you I was well-pleased" (aorist); LOGOS: "in you I am well pleased" (English present); PNEUMA: "In you I am well pleased." The Greek EUDOKHSA is aorist indicative. SOPHIA adopts "in you I am well pleased" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) as the conventional English rendering of this divine declaration, noting the aorist in the disagreement log.

**v. 23:** All three agree on "about thirty years." EMET's "[to be]" and "[his ministry]" are supplied; LOGOS supplies "of age" and "his ministry"; PNEUMA supplies "old" and "his ministry." SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

**v. 30:** EMET: "Symeon"; LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Simeon." SOPHIA uses "Simeon" (majority, standard English form).

### Manuscript Verification
The Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 87-88) confirm the readings adopted. Page 87 (LUK_003_page_001.jpg) shows columns beginning at Lk 2:45, with Lk 3:1 starting in the second column and continuing through approximately Lk 3:15 in the fourth column. Page 88 (LUK_003_page_002.jpg) shows the baptism narrative (3:21ff) and the genealogy running through columns 1-2, with Luke 4 beginning in column 3. The nomina sacra, itacisms, and genealogical variants (Admin, Arni, Kainam) are visible in the manuscript and have been correctly handled by all three translators.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Severity |
|-------|-------|-----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|----------|
| 2 | Preposition for high priesthood | "upon" (epi) | "during" | restructured away | "during" (LOGOS) — EPI + genitive = temporal | Minor |
| 2 | "Zacharias" vs. "Zechariah" | Zacharias | Zechariah | Zechariah | Zechariah (majority, standard English) | Minor |
| 3 | Baptism description | "proclaiming a baptism of repentance unto forgiveness" | "proclaiming a baptism of repentance for the forgiveness" | "calling people to be baptized as a sign that they had turned from their sins" | LOGOS — PNEUMA paraphrases too freely | Moderate |
| 4 | Isaiah citation formula | "has been written" | "is written" | "fulfilled what was written" | "is written" (LOGOS) — standard perfect tense rendering; PNEUMA adds "fulfilled" not in text | Moderate |
| 6 | "all flesh" vs. "all humanity" | "all flesh" | "all flesh" | "all humanity" | "all flesh" (EMET/LOGOS) — preserves biblical idiom | Minor |
| 7 | "Offspring" vs. "brood" | "Offspring" | "offspring" | "brood" | "offspring" — closer to GENNHMATA | Minor |
| 8 | Singular vs. plural "fruit(s)" | "fruits" (plural) | "fruits" (plural) | "fruit" (singular) | "fruits" (EMET/LOGOS) — Sinaiticus reads KARPOUS (plural) | Minor |
| 8 | "to Abraham" vs. "for Abraham" | "to Abraham" | "to Abraham" | "for Abraham" | "for Abraham" (PNEUMA) — dative of advantage | Minor |
| 9 | "cast into fire" vs. "thrown into the fire" | "cast into fire" | "thrown into the fire" | "thrown into the fire" | "thrown into the fire" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) | Minor |
| 15 | "Christ" vs. "Messiah" | "the Christ" | "the Christ" | "the Messiah" | "the Christ" (EMET/LOGOS) — preserves Greek XPISTOS as title | Minor |
| 17 | "grain" vs. "wheat" | "grain" | "wheat" | "wheat" | "wheat" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — SITON = wheat specifically | Minor |
| 17 | "burn up" vs. "burn" | "burn up" | "burn" | "burn" | "burn" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) | Minor |
| 19 | Reproof description | "being reproved by him" | "being reproved by him" | "when John rebuked Herod" | "being reproved by him" (EMET/LOGOS) — preserves participial structure | Minor |
| 21 | Heaven "opened" vs. "torn open" | "was opened" | "was opened" | "was torn open" | "was opened" (EMET/LOGOS) — Sinaiticus reads ANOIXQHNAI not Mark's SXIZOMENOUS | Moderate |
| 22 | Aorist tense of EUDOKHSA | "I was well-pleased" (aorist) | "I am well pleased" (present) | "I am well pleased" (present) | "I am well pleased" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — conventional English; noted aorist | Minor |
| 30 | Name spelling | "Symeon" | "Simeon" | "Simeon" | "Simeon" (majority) | Trivial |
